title: Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports
caption:
Plastic waste per capita imports vs. exports, 2024
Imports per person (tonnes; plotted on a logarithmic axis)

This scatter plot maps the relationship between plastic waste imports and exports per person for countries in 2024, using logarithmic scales on both axes to display values ranging from 0.000001 to over 10 tonnes. Each point represents a nation, colored by income group, revealing a wide dispersion of trade balances. A diagonal line marks the point where imports equal exports; points above this line import more than they export, while points below export more than they import. High-income countries, shown in dark green, occupy diverse positions. Austria and Norway appear in the upper right, with exports exceeding 10 tonnes per person and imports between 10 and 20 tonnes. Conversely, Iceland and French Polynesia sit in the lower right, exporting between 1 and 10 tonnes per person while importing less than 0.01 tonnes. The Bahamas and Malaysia show high import levels around 10 tonnes with lower export volumes. Upper-middle-income countries, in light green, include Turkey and Azerbaijan, which export between 0.1 and 1 tonne per person while importing roughly 1 to 10 tonnes. China and Ecuador appear near the bottom, with exports around 0.1 to 1 tonne and imports below 0.001 tonnes. Lower-middle-income countries, in pink, include Nepal and Cote d'Ivoire at the lowest end of the scale, with both imports and exports below 0.0001 tonnes per person. Afghanistan and Peru show higher import levels relative to their exports. Low-income countries, in purple, cluster in the lower left, with the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Madagascar showing imports and exports below 0.01 tonnes. The data shows no single linear relationship, with nations distributed across all quadrants of the trade balance spectrum.
